coredata.dc:description: Indoor formaldehyde pollution released from household products is one of the major causes of several health diseases. Ornamental plants have been paid more attention recently to improve air quality and increase natural decorative appeal. Therefore, we aimed to use ornamental plants as a plant-based biosensor or phytosensor to monitor/detect indoor formaldehyde exposure. The synthesized carbon dots with nitrogen doping (N-doped CDs) in this study shows a big “turn-off” significant response with high selectivity to formaldehyde. The lowest formaldehyde concentration that gave the observable fluorescence quenching signal by naked eye on Fittonia albivenis (Nerve Plant) was 50.0 μg/L, which is lower than the exposure limit of 80.0 μg/L as indicated by World Health Organization. The results showed that ornamental plants with the aid of N-doped CDs can be developed into phytosensor for formaldehyde detection. Furthermore, findings in this study will be useful for the development of plant biosensor for other indoor air pollution monitoring.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: N-doped CDs synthesis. (A) Schematic of N-doped CDs formation using citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as substrates. (B-C) N-doped CDs solution under (B) white light and (C) UV irradiation at λex = 365 nm.

Optical characteristics of synthesized N-doped CDs. (A) Absorption (blue), excitation (green), and emission (red) spectra of N-doped CDs. (B) Emission spectrum of N-doped CDs excited from 300 nm to 380 nm.

Fluorescence response of N-doped CDs solution (50.0 μg/L, λ ex = 360 nm) to formaldehyde solution with concentration ranging from 0.0 to 7200.0 μg/L). Fluorescence intensity declines as formaldehyde concentration increases.

Fluorescent response of N-doped CDs to different type of VOCs and common organic solvents. (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-doped CDs in the presence of various type of VOCs and common organic solvents. (B) Fluorescence changes of N-doped CDs in methanol/water (1:1 v/v) solution with addition of various type of VOCs and common organic solvents as observed by naked eye under UV irradiation. The fluorescence change visible by naked eye was obtained only from the sample with formaldehyde addition.

Proposed quenching mechanisms of N-doped CDs solution after being exposed to formaldehyde. Upon the presence of formaldehyde, amino group at the surface of CDs can react with carbonyl carbon resulting in the formation of either imine or amino methanol, thereby, quenching the emission of fluorescence signal.
Napa cabbage immersed in water and N-doped CDs solution under (A) white light and (B) UV irradiation.
Cross section and longitudinal section of napa cabbage leaf immersed in N-doped CDs solution. Numbers under each laser filter indicate excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively.

Effect of formaldehyde on napa cabbage leaf after immersed in N-doped CDs solution. Water and sample without formaldehyde fuming were used as negative controls. Red circles indicate the detected quenched signal at leaf margin.

Effect of formaldehyde on F. albivenis leaves after being sprayed by N-doped CDs solution. (A) F. albivenis without N-doped CDs. (B) Fluorescent emission signal after placed the plants in sealed desiccator containing varied concentration of formaldehyde ranging from 0.0 to 400.0 μg/L as observed by naked eye under UV irradiation.

Examples of previously reported chemosensors for formaldehyde detection. Only some reports relating to the development of formaldehyde chemosensors using small molecules or quantum dots as a base of detection and published during 2019–2020 were selected.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots based phytosensor for indoor formaldehyde pollution monitoring

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Indoor formaldehyde pollution released from household products is one of the major causes of several health diseases. Ornamental plants have been paid more attention recently to improve air quality and increase natural decorative appeal. Therefore, we aimed to use ornamental plants as a plant-based biosensor or phytosensor to monitor/detect indoor formaldehyde exposure. The synthesized carbon dots with nitrogen doping (N-doped CDs) in this study shows a big “turn-off” significant response with high selectivity to formaldehyde. The lowest formaldehyde concentration that gave the observable fluorescence quenching signal by naked eye on Fittonia albivenis (Nerve Plant) was 50.0 μg/L, which is lower than the exposure limit of 80.0 μg/L as indicated by World Health Organization. The results showed that ornamental plants with the aid of N-doped CDs can be developed into phytosensor for formaldehyde detection. Furthermore, findings in this study will be useful for the development of plant biosensor for other indoor air pollution monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Indoor air pollution is a complex mixture of gas pollutants and particles that frequently causes by indoor human activities and the release from building materials and furniture with poor ventilation (Lin et al., 2022). One of the indoor gas pollutants, a colorless formaldehyde gas is released from wood and leather furniture, plastics, fiberboard, particle board, and other household products. It is one of the major air components that causes health effects and has either short- or long-term harmful sick building syndrome (Liteplo and Meek, 2003; Cao and Zhang, 2011). Formaldehyde can induce amnesia and cause DNA damage that soon will lead to cancer (Merk and Speit, 1998). It is also a major harm to eyes, nose, mouth, throat, and skin (Sakai et al., 2004; Feng et al., 2005; Kim et al., 2011; Wei et al., 2017). Long exposure to high doses of formaldehyde can result in death (Zhao and Zhang, 2009). In response to the high toxicity of formaldehyde, excessive contaminant indoor formaldehyde exposure limit of 80.0 μg/L for 1 h and 0.15 mg/kg of weight in daily routine has been approved by the World Health Organization (WHO) (WHO Regional Office for Europe, 2001). The United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) allows the acceptable maximum limit without a risk of deleterious noncancer effects for formaldehyde exposure at a 0.2 mg/kg of weight a day (U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (US-EPA), 1990).

Several methods for formaldehyde detection have been reported. These include gas chromatography (Gu et al., 2010; Antwi-Boampong and BelBruno, 2013), liquid chromatography (Liu et al., 2005; Allouch et al., 2013), Raman spectroscopy (Zhang et al., 2014), X-ray diffraction (XRD), amperometry or conductive measurements (Kataky et al., 2002), quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) measurement (Zhu et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2014), piezoresistive sensor (Bunde et al., 2000), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Chung et al., 2014), and formaldehyde dehydrogenase-based biosensor (Dennison et al., 1996; Demkiv et al., 2008; Mitsubayashi et al., 2008). However, these methods are costly, inconvenient, inadequate, and/or require the services of advance-trained operators (Chung et al., 2013). Different sensing techniques are also needed for the detection of formaldehyde in real time (Chen et al., 2014; Zeng et al., 2014). Recently, carbon dots (CDs), a type of carbon-based materials, have attracted a great attention due to their potential applications in a wide range of areas including chemo- and biosensing, cellular imaging, and biolabeling (Yuan et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2018). CDs exhibit excellence photoluminescence with high quantum yield, low/nontoxicity, and good biocompatibility (Gao et al., 2016; Dong et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020). They can also be prepared using a low cost of starting materials (Liu et al., 2020). From these unique and diverse physicochemical properties, CDs show a great potential to be developed into a low cost yet effective sensor for formaldehyde gas detection.

Indoor ornamental plants are of interests and have entered a globalized market over the past decade. They have decoratively visual attributes and beneficial effects on mental, physiological, and social well-being (Symoneaux et al., 2022). Many of those also show phytoremediation capability to reduce such heavy metals, organic chemicals, and air pollution contaminants (Rocha et al., 2021). These characteristics have led to the increasing of the number of consumers over time and inspired us to improve their values even further, in particular, as a plant-based biosensor for formaldehyde gas detection.

As fluorescent biosensor has high sensitivity, high selectivity, and rapid detection (Mankoff, 2007; Lv et al., 2008), we integrated fluorescent CDs with ornamental plants to generate the plant-based biosensor, or as we called phytosensor, to specifically detect formaldehyde gas. The synthesized CDs exhibit a “turn-off” fluorescence response and have a good cell permeability. After treated our model ornamental plant with these CDs, we found that the quenched fluorescent signal was detectable even with the concentration of formaldehyde lower than the maximum exposure limit of indoor formaldehyde contamination as suggested by WHO. Our study suggests that the developed phytosensor has a great potential to be used as a highly effective indoor gas detector, which in turn, can promote the values of widely attractive ornamental plants.

All chemicals for synthesis and sensing test were purchased from Aldrich (Missouri, US). All of them were analytical grade and used as received without purification. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments. Napa cabbage and Fittonia albivenis were purchased from local stores in Bangkok, Thailand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: N-doped CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal synthesis following the previous report (Zhu et al., 2013). In brief, in a Teflon liner, 0.42 g citric acid monohydrate (0.42 g, 2.0 mmol) and ethylenediamine (0.15 mL, 2.2 mmol) were dissolved in 10.0 mL DI water. The Teflon liner was placed in an autoclave and heated for 5 h at 180 °C. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the reaction products were filtered using a syringe filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to separate N-doped CDs from agglomerated particles and redissolved in DI water for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization, absorption, and fluorescence analyses of N-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum and fluorescence study of the N-doped CDs were analyzed using Cytation 5 Cell Imaging Multi-Mode Reader (BioTek, Vermont, US) The excitation wavelength (λex) was set at 360 nm, the slit widths of emission and excitation wavelength were both 10.0 nm. HR-TEM image was recorded on JEM-2100Plus transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were determined using a Malvern Zeta Potential Analyzer with Zetasizer version 7.01 software (Malvern, UK). Scanning electron microscope image and energy dispersive X-ray spectrum (SEM-EDS) of N-doped CDs were analyzed using Thermo Fisher Scientific Phenom ProX (Massachusetts, US). Fourier-transform infrared spectrum was analyzed using FT-IR spectrometer Invenio (Bruker, Massachusetts, US). Nuclear magnetic resonance spectrum was analyzed using Bruker Avance III HD 400 MHz NMR Spectrometer (Massachusetts, US).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formaldehyde detection using N-doped carbon dots solution
ce:section.ce:para: The formaldehyde detection experiments were performed by mixing 200.0 μL of 50.0 μg/L N-doped CDs solution with 10.0 μL of formaldehyde at varied concentrations (0.0, 18.0, 36.0, 72.0, 108.0, 180.0, 270.0, 360.0, 720.0, 1080.0, 1800.0, 2700.0, 3600.0, 4500.0, 5400.0, and 7200.0 μg/L). For selectivity analysis, 10.0 μL of VOCs and common organic solvents including THF, acetonitrile, acetone, DMF, ethanol, methanol, hexane, toluene, benzene, ethylene glycol, dichrolomethane, ethyl acetate, and xylene were added to N-doped CDs solution. Water addition was used as a negative control in this study. All fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature on Cytation 5. The sensor was excited at λex= 360 nm with slit widths ex = 10.0/em = 10.0 nm. The emission spectrum was scanned from 380 nm to 700 nm.

Quantum yield (Φ) of N-doped CDs was calculated using a comparative method following the previous report (Dai et al., 2014). Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index (η) = 1.33) with literature quantum yield = 54 % was used as a fluorescent standard molecule (Dernas and Crosby, 1971; Heller et al., 1974). The absorbance values and fluorescence emission spectrum of the solutions being excited at 360 nm were recorded. The fluorescence quantum yield was then determined using the following equation:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of formaldehyde on fluorescent emission in napa cabbage treated with N-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Napa cabbages were immersed in 50.0 mg/L N-doped CDs solution for 6 h at 25 °C under natural light condition. Then they were placed in the sealed desiccator (2.5 L) containing 200.0 μg/L formaldehyde for 1 h. After incubation with formaldehyde for 1 h. The resulted napa cabbages were photographs under white light and UV irradiation. Napa cabbages immersed in water and N-doped CDs without formaldehyde gas fuming were used as negative controls. For cellular imaging of N-doped CDs in napa cabbage leaf, napa cabbage leaf of sample immersed in N-doped CDs without formaldehyde gas fuming was thoroughly washed by DI water to remove the residual solution. Then cross and longitudinal sections of the leaf were cut and analyzed by Cytation 5 using DAPI, CFP, and CY5 laser filters for excitations at 377, 445, and 628 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of formaldehyde on fluorescent emission in Fittonia albivenis treated with N-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: N-doped CDs solution (50.0 mg/L) was poured to the tree base or sprayed to the leaves of F. albivenis. After pouring/spraying for 1 h, the plants were placed in the sealed desiccator (2.5 L) containing 100.0 μg/L formaldehyde for 1 h. The plants were then photographed under white light and UV irradiation. For the analysis of minimum formaldehyde concentration detectability, after spraying N-doped CDs solution (50.0 mg/L) to F. albivenis for 1 h, the plants were placed in the sealed desiccator (2.5 L) containing varied concentration of formaldehyde (0.0, 50.0, 100.0, 200.0, and 400.0 μg/L) for 1 h before being photographed under white light and UV light (365 nm). Fluorescence intensity of each sample was recorded and reported after being subtracted with the intensity of the sample treated with water as a background negative control in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The development of formaldehyde gas chemosensor, using either small molecules or quantum dots as a based of detection, has been studied over the past few years. Some of which are shown in Table 1. Among these, those based on the formation of imine have been reported (Bi et al., 2017). Regarding these reports together with key characteristics of CDs as a highly luminous and low cytotoxic material, we speculated that N-doped CDs could be a promising sensor for the detection of formaldehyde as well. Therefore, we chemically synthesized N-doped CDs via hydrothermal synthesis using citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as starting materials ( Fig. 1A) (Zhu et al., 2013). As shown in Figs. 1B and 1C, N-doped CDs solution has brownish color but emits a bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (λex = 365 nm). HR-TEM image shows that N-doped CDs have dark nanospherical shape and highly monodispersed particles with mean diameter of 4.0 nm and zeta potential of − 4.49 mV at pH ∼7 (Figs. S1 and S2). SEM-EDS elemental mapping also reveal the homogenous distribution of C, O, and N in N-doped CDs (Fig. S3).

Next, we investigated the optical characteristics of N-doped CDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, UV–visible spectrum of our synthetic N-doped CDs has two distinct peaks at 258 and 360 nm (blue line). The difference in absorption intensity between these two peaks indicates the strong absorption at 258 nm of π–π * transition of CC bonds in sp2 carbon atom on the surface of CDs (Zhu et al., 2013). The weak absorption at 360 nm of n–π * transition of C-N and C-O bonds reveals that nitrogen- and oxygen-atoms are present on the surface or in the center of the molecule (Subramanina et al., 2003; Paredes et al., 2008; Zhu et al., 2010). This result is in accordance with the surface functional group analysis using FT-IR. Pure CA exhibited characteristic absorption bands of –COOH at υ = 3288 cm−1 (O-H stretching), 1722 cm−1 (CO stretching), 1211 cm−1 (C-O stretching of tertiary alcohol), and 1106 cm−1 (C-O stretching of C(O)-OH) (Fig. S4A) (Pimpang et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. S4B, FTIR spectrum of N-doped CDs exhibited the wide band withing the range of 2700 cm−1 to 3400 cm−1 (O-H and N-H stretching), 1531 cm−1 (N-H bending) and 1375 cm−1 (C-N stretching) (Cloos and Laura, 1972; Xu et al., 2019). Despite being weaker bands, most characteristic absorption bands of CA still appeared in the spectrum of N-doped CDs. However, the band at 1210 cm−1 was absent. These results indicate that the surface of N-doped CDs was functionalized with –COOH and –NH2 groups.

To analyze the excitation and emission maxima of N-doped CDs, we varied the excitation wavelength from 300 to 380 nm and observed the emission spectrum from 380 to 650 nm. As shown in Fig. 2B, we found that the luminous intensity of N-doped CDs is excitation wavelength dependence where the emission wavelength is red-shifting as the excitation wavelength increases. The highest fluorescence emission peak (at 450 nm) was obtained when stimulated N-doped CDs at 360 nm. Thereby, these excitation and emission wavelengths of N-doped CDs were focused in the following experiment. Green and red lines in Fig. 2A are excitation and emission spectra of N-doped CDs, respectively. Quantum yield of N-doped CDs was calculated using Eq. 1 (as shown in Section 2) and found to be 78.8 % as compared to quinine sulfate, a fluorescent standard molecule (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance analysis of N-doped carbon dots for formaldehyde detection
ce:section.ce:para: To test the performance of the synthesized N-doped CDs, we mixed N-doped CDs with varied concentrations of formaldehyde and observed the change in fluorescence emission intensity. As shown in Fig. 3, fluorescence intensity of N-doped CDs dramatically decreased with higher concentration of formaldehyde. We also found that fluorescence response of N-doped CDs has a linear relationship with formaldehyde concentration over the range of 36.0 μg/L to 270.0 μg/L (Fig. S6). Thereby, we used this calibration curve to calculate the theoretical limit of detection (LOD) based on the standard deviation of blank measurement using Eq. 2 (as shown in Section 2) and found that theoretical LOD of our N-doped CDs is 22.0 μg/L which is lower than that of the WHO standard (WHO Regional Office for Europe, 2001).

To further analyze the selectivity of N-doped CDs, we mixed various types of indoor volatile organic compounds (VOCs) including some common organic solvents (Nazaroff and Weschler, 2004; Gallego et al., 2009; Zhong et al., 2017; Lee et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 4A, fluorescence emission intensity of N-doped CDs greatly reduced after mixed with formaldehyde. Acetone and THF could moderately quench the emission fluorescence intensity of N-doped CDs. Although mixing N-doped CDs with other tested chemicals showed slightly decrease in fluorescence intensity, no significant difference could be detected by naked eye (Fig. 4B). This result shows that the synthesized N-doped CDs are highly selective to formaldehyde detection.

Upon interaction with formaldehyde, we could detect imine and amino methanol groups at the surface of the synthesized N-doped CDs (Fig. S7). Several studies showed that one of the key factors involving in the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs is their surface functional groups (Pan et al., 2021). Thereby, changes in the surface functional groups can either enhance or quench the emission signal (Song et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2015; Dong et al., 2016; Tang et al., 2016; Xia et al., 2016). From these, we can speculate that the “turn-off” sensing mechanism of our CDs could undergo in the same manner i.e., via nucleophilic addition of lone pair electron of amino group on the surface of N-doped CDs to carbonyl group of formaldehyde ( Fig. 5). This phenomenon can also occur when acetone was used as a target of detection (Fresneau et al. 2014). Still, acetone may not be a suitable target due to its lower electrophilicity at carbonyl carbon as compared to formaldehyde (Neuyonen et al., 2002). The reaction between surface functionalized amino group and THF could lead to pyrrolidine formation (Eicher et al., 2003). However, this reaction usually occurs at harsh condition, thereby, the selectivity of N-doped CDs against THF is likely to be unfavored as compared to the reaction against formaldehyde.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular imaging and fluorescence emission of N-doped carbon dots in napa cabbage leaf
ce:section.ce:para: We selected napa cabbage leaf (Brassica pekinensis L.) as a model plant to study the uptake and transportation of N-doped CDs. As the color of napa cabbage leaf is white, it may ensure the signal visibility without any interference from the chlorophyll. We immersed napa cabbage leaf in N-doped CDs solution and observed the fluorescence emission signal from the plant. As shown in Fig. 6, napa cabbage leaf emitted bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation as compared to untreated one. Then we analyzed the cellular imaging of the leaf in both cross and longitudinal sections using various filters. As shown in Fig. 7, clear emission signal of N-doped CDs was detected inside cell wall and cell membrane of napa cabbage leaf when we used DAPI as excitation filter at 377 nm. These results show that N-doped CDs can be absorbed probably through the cabbage core then diffuse across the cells indicating a good cell permeability. However, when CFP and CY5 were used as excitation filters at 445 nm and 628 nm, respectively, emission signal of N-doped CDs was almost undetected. This finding clearly shows that excitation wavelength in visible light region is not suitable for N-doped CDs coinciding with their optical characteristics as shown in Fig. 2.

After fumed formaldehyde to napa cabbage leaf treated with N-doped CDs, we found that bright blue fluorescence signal emitted at the leaf margin was quenched as compared to the controls while the signal at the rib was almost unchanged ( Fig. 8). As permeability decreases with the thickness, formaldehyde may not be able to reach and interact with N-doped CDs located at the leaf rib area. Although, N-doped CDs may have good cell permeability and are able to translocate across the cells, plant cell thickness becomes one of the major drawbacks for their sensing capability. With aiming for using these CDs with ornamental plants, therefore, thin cell thickness ornamental plants are needed. Otherwise, other methods to apply N-doped CDs at the outermost layer of the plants are required.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of formaldehyde gas using Fittonia albivenis treated with N-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: As chlorophyll absorbs photoluminescence within the range of 450 nm, fluorescence emission signal of N-doped CDs in ornamental plants containing chlorophyll may be obscured. To prove this, Fittonia albivenis (Nerve Plant) was selected as a model ornamental plant. We poured N-doped CDs solution and allowed the plant to absorb and translocate N-doped CDs from its roots to the leaves. The other set of experiment was conducted by spraying N-doped CDs directly to the leaves surface to ensure the detectable fluorescence without any absorption interference from chlorophyll. As expected, we could not detect any fluorescence signal from N-doped CDs pouring sample while bright blue signal was obtained from the latter one (Fig. S8). This result confirms our speculation that the emitted fluorescence of N-doped CDs at 450 nm can be absorbed by chlorophyll. Therefore, spraying N-doped CDs on the leaves surface to generate ornamental plant biosensor may be more suitable method.

To analyze the minimum concentration of formaldehyde that can be detected by N-doped CDs on leaves surface, we sprayed N-doped CDs to F. albivenis leaves and placed the plant in a sealed desiccator before fuming formaldehyde at different concentration ranging from 0.0 to 400.0 μg/L. After 1 h of fuming, we found that emitted fluorescence intensity gradually decreased logarithmically ( Fig. 9 and S9). The signal is barely detected at 400.0 μg/L formaldehyde, and the difference as compared to the control can be observed by naked eye since 50.0 μg/L formaldehyde which is lower than the indoor exposure limit of this toxic gas as suggested by WHO (WHO Regional Office for Europe, 2001). This result shows that our synthesized N-doped CDs have quick response towards indoor formaldehyde gas contamination. Although N-doped CDs emit fluorescence at the same absorption wavelength of chlorophyll, this can also overcome by spraying N-doped CDs on the leaves surface instead of applying them from inside the plant cells.

In the present, people tend to spend most of their time in their residents, offices, institutes, and other buildings. However, due to inadequate ventilation, air pollution caused by building materials and furniture gradually causes a huge impact on human health and becomes one of the main reasons for disability and deaths (Mannan and Al-Ghamdi, 2021). In response to this, we attempted to develop a sensing device based on indoor ornament for indoor air pollution detection. As shown in this study, we chemically synthesized N-doped CDs with highly selective to toxic formaldehyde gas. The visible “switch-off” fluorescence signal of N-doped CDs on F. albivenis leaves surface when being exposed to formaldehyde was 50.0 μg/L which is much lower than the maximum exposure limit of indoor air pollution contaminants as guided by WHO (WHO Regional Office for Europe, 2001). This development herein enriches the sensor device for air pollution detection/monitoring.

Plants have been paid more attention recently as a platform for phytosensor development as they can grow in a high-risk location and can provide a real time monitoring of agriculture and environmental changes (Kwak et al., 2017). Such developments include the monitoring of crop growth, biomass enhancement, plant metabolites production detection, water, soil, and air qualities monitoring, smart farming, and stresses monitoring (Kwak et al., 2017; Shang et al., 2019; Dufil et al., 2022). In contrast to outdoor plants, ornamental plants are generally used to decorate household and improve indoor air quality using their phytoremediation capability (Rocha et al., 2021). Regarding this purpose, many current studies have made efforts to enhance this phytoremediation capacity of ornamental plants either using microsymbionts or other environmental factors (Franzetti et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). Still several factors regarding plant survival after being exposed to such contaminants have to be concerned otherwise negative influences on ornamental plants can be occurred.

F. albivenis is a small flowering perennials plant. It is of interest as ornamental plant due to its rich green leaves with high varieties of color in its veins. In this study, we have developed formaldehyde responsive F. albivenis plant with the aid of N-doped CDs. Although several studies on formaldehyde detection device development have been reported (Bi et al., 2017), many of these could detect the contaminant only in the liquid phase and/or could not observe the signal read out with naked eye. In contrast, our study shows the potential to integrate ornamental plants and CDs for indoor air pollution monitorable by naked eye with limit of detection below the maximum exposure limit according to WHO guideline. Still fluorescence emission signal of our N-doped CDs was the same wavelength as absorption band of chlorophyll. This becomes a drawback for using this platform as the signal readout can be obscured. Thereby, spraying N-doped CDs on the plant leaves surface is needed. To overcome this, formaldehyde responsive CDs with different emission wavelength are needed. Highly fluorescent probe that can automatically emit the signal without the requirement of UV irradiation may also be of use as the signal can be recognized easier. Nonetheless, our finding paves the way to promote the use of ornamental plants as phytosensor for toxic gas contamination monitoring.

P1851883) to W.S. and postdoctoral fellowship from King Mongkut’s University of Technology Thonburi to Y.T.
